coredata.dc:title: Application of N-doped carbon quantum dots as a resonance light scattering probe for determination of fluvoxamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Fazl, Fariba
Bagher Gholivand, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Resonance light scattering (RLS) as a simple, rapid, inexpensive method was introduced for the determination of fluvoxamine in real samples (blood serum and commercial tablet) based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) probe. The N-CQDs were characterized using various techniques such as UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), which revealed their size distribution range to be between 1 and 6 nm. The optimized conditions resulted in a linear response between 0.01 and 12.0 mg L-1 and a limit of detection of 5 µg/L, making this method suitable for analyzing fluvoxamine in blood serum and commercial tablet samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Resonance light scattering
N-doped carbon quantum dots
Drug monitoring
Fluvoxamine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of fluvoxamine in real samples
Comparison of the efficiency of the present method with the reported methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) The absorption spectrum (blue line) and the fluorescence spectrum (red line), b) TEM image, c) DLS spectrum, d) XRD pattern, e) FTIR spectrum corresponding to N-CQDs, and f) fluorescence decay profile.
RLS spectra of N-CQDs in the presence (a) and absence (b) of fluvoxamine.
Influence of the solution pH on the RLS signal intensities. Experimental conditions: fluvoxamine concentration (1.0 mg L-1), probe concentration (40.0 mg L-1) and ultrasonication time (5 min).
Influence of the probe concentration on the RLS signal intensities. Experimental conditions: fluvoxamine concentration (1.0 mg L-1), ultrasonication time (5 min) and pH = 7.
Influence of the ultrasonication time on the RLS signal intensities. Experimental conditions: fluvoxamine concentration (1.0 mg L-1), probe concentration (60 mg L-1) and pH = 7.
A) influence of various concentrations of fluvoxamine on the rls spectra and b) the calibration curve under the optimized conditions.
Interference of some coexisting substances for 4.0 mg L-1 of fluvoxamine.
Spiked-recovery of fluvoxamine in serum samples.
Determination results of fluvoxamine in drug tablet samples.
Analytical parameters of the present method and previous methods for measuring fluvoxamine.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Microextraction by packed sorbent-ultra performance liquid chromatography-photodiode array.

head.ce:title: Application of N-doped carbon quantum dots as a resonance light scattering probe for determination of fluvoxamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resonance light scattering (RLS) as a simple, rapid, inexpensive method was introduced for the determination of fluvoxamine in real samples (blood serum and commercial tablet) based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) probe. The N-CQDs were characterized using various techniques such as UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), which revealed their size distribution range to be between 1 and 6 nm. The optimized conditions resulted in a linear response between 0.01 and 12.0 mg L-1 and a limit of detection of 5 µg/L, making this method suitable for analyzing fluvoxamine in blood serum and commercial tablet samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluvoxamine (shown in Scheme 1 ) is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) of the second generation that has been approved for treating various disorders, including depression, social phobia, anxiety, eating disorders, posttraumatic stress disorder, and obsessive–compulsive disorder [1–3]. With the increase in incidents of fluvoxamine poisoning and the lack of quick testing methods, there is a growing need for a cheap and fast way to test this drug [4,5]. Several analytical methods, such as chromatography [6], spectroscopy [7], and electrochemistry [8,9] have been used to determine this antidepressant in biological samples. However, these methods are costly, time-consuming, use toxic solvents, and are not practical for routine testing [10,11]. In contrast, the resonance light scattering (RLS) technique is a simple, fast, and powerful alternative based on elastic light scattering that can be used for fluvoxamine testing [12,13].

Resonance light scattering (RLS) has gained popularity among analytical physicists and chemists due to its numerous advantages, such as high sensitivity, speed, simplicity, and convenience (using a commonly available spectrofluorimeter) [14]. It is a versatile method that can be applied to various interactions, including intermolecular electrostatic attraction, hydrophobic interaction, and macromolecule aggregation interaction [15]. Recently, researchers have employed the RLS technique to determine nucleic acids, nanoparticles, proteins, inorganic ions, and other substances [16–19]. This demonstrates the broad applicability and potential of RLS in analytical chemistry.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of nanomaterials made of zero-dimensional carbon with a size smaller than 20 nm. They possess outstanding properties such as good biocompatibility, high stability at high temperatures, easy surface modification, high quantum yield, low toxicity, and abundant precursor sources [20–23], which make them attractive for a variety of applications such as photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, sensing, drug delivery, energy conversion/storage, hydrogen evolution and bioimaging [24–34]. Heteroatom doping can change the electronic density of CQDs, thereby tuning their optical and electrical properties. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have been found to have enhanced catalytic ability and higher electronic conductivity than undoped CQDs, resulting in an increase in the active sites as pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N [35]. Previous studies have shown that the interaction between drugs and N-CQDs is stronger than the interaction between drugs and undoped CQDs [36]. Furthermore, increasing the interaction between the adsorbent and substrate can increase RLS intensity [17]. Due to these unique properties, N-CQDs were chosen for investigation in this study. Our objective in the present work is to use the light-scattering properties of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) as a probe for the determination of fluvoxamine.

The analytical-grade chemicals with no further purification were used in this study. Glucuronic acid and N-methylethylenediamine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Company (USA). Briton-Robinson buffer (BRB) solutions were prepared by combining appropriate volumes of 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M phosphoric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid, and then adjusting to the desired pH with 0.2 M NaOH. All chemicals used for preparing BRB were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Company. Fluvoxamine tablets (50 mg and 100 mg, Jalinous Pharmaceutical Co., Iran) were obtained from the local drug store.

The samples underwent various characterization techniques. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to study their crystal structure over the 2θ range of 10°-60°, using a Bruker D8 advance XRD diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). For imaging the samples, a transmission electron microscope (TEM) with Philips CM30 using an accelerating voltage of 100 kV was utilized. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to identify the surface functionalities of the samples, and the spectra were recorded in the range of 400 to 4000 cm−1, by a Bruker tensor 27 instrument. The pH of the solutions was measured with a Metrohm pH meter (model 727), while a ROHS ultrasonic water bath was used at 40 kHz. For recording the RLS spectra, a luminescence spectrometer (LS50B, Perkin-Elmer) was used. Finally, the size distribution of the N-CQDs in water was determined by using dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a Zetasizer Nano (ZEN3600).

The N-CQDs were prepared using a hydrothermal process from a combination of glucuronic acid as the carbon source and N-methylethylenediamine as the nitrogen source. The process involved dissolving 1 g of glucuronic acid and 2 mL of N-methylethylenediamine in 10 mL of purified water with stirring. The resulting solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h before being allowed to cool to room temperature. The dark brown solution obtained was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 15 min to remove any particles. Further purification was carried out by using a 0.2 μm membrane filter [37]. After evaporation of the solvent, the purified N-CQDs were finally dried in an oven at 105 ℃ for 12 h.

The determination of the fluorescence quantum yield for the N-GQDs involved a four-step optical process: 1) Assessing the absorption and emission spectrum of the N-GQDs. 2) Selecting an appropriate fluorescence quantum yield standard based on specific measurement conditions such as solvent/matrix, excitation wavelength, and temperature. 3) Choosing the excitation wavelength (λex) and absorbance spectra at λex for both the sample and standard. 4) Evaluating data and computing the relative fluorescence quantum yield using the following Equation [38,39]:
(1)
Φ
f , x
=
Φ
f , s t
.
F x
F
st
.
f
st
(
λ
ex
)
f x
( λ )
.
n
x
2
n
st
2
In this equation, the sample and standard are denoted as 'x' and 'st', respectively. 'F' represents the spectrally integrated photon flux, the area under the emission spectrum of both the sample and standard. The equation also includes a refractive index (n2), applicable if different solvents are utilized for the sample and standard. The absorption factor f (λex) indicates the fraction of excitation light absorbed by the sample and standard. The quantum yield of the N-GQDs was determined following a well-established procedure. Fluorescein in 0.10 mol/L NaOH at λex = 507 nm, with fst = 407.47 and Fst = 73344.6, was chosen as the standard. The N-GQDs in 0.10 mol/L NaOH were selected as the sample. Applying Equation (1), the quantum yield of the N-GQDs was determined to be 69 %.
We conducted a cycling test (four cycles) for the fluorescent probes, and the results show the stability of the probes over repeated cycles.

To prepare the samples for RLS spectroscopy, a specified volume of the sample was mixed with 1 mL of Briton-Robinson buffer (BRB) and 0.5 mL of 60 mg L-1N-CQDs in 5 mL volumetric flasks, which were then filled with purified water. The resulting solutions were ultrasonicated for 12 min and the RLS spectra were measured using a spectrofluorometer with excitation and emission monochromators set to the same wavelength between 200 and 800 nm. The slit widths were set to 10 nm. The RLS intensity change (ΔIRLS) was calculated by subtracting the intensity of the blank (I0) from that of the system (IRLS) at 434 nm (ΔIRLS = IRLS − I0).

Healthy volunteer serum samples were collected and kept refrigerated at 4 °C. Different amounts of fluvoxamine (20 and 50 µg/L) were spiked to 1 mL of the serum samples. Next, 8 mL of acetonitrile was mixed with the sample for 5 min and then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. After centrifugation, 50 mL of BRB with a pH of 9.5 was added to the sample, and then the mixture was transferred to a volumetric flask without any further treatment. For preparing the tablet samples, ten fluvoxamine tablets were crushed to obtain a homogenous powder. A part of the obtained powder equivalent to the weight of one pill was added to a 25 mL volumetric flask and dissolved in purified water. The solution was sonicated for 5 min and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained solution was then filtered and diluted to 100 mL volumetric with BRB.

This study utilized glucuronic acid and N-methylethylenediamine as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, to synthesize the N-CQDs. The N-CQDs underwent several types of characterization, including UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, TEM, DLS, XRD, and FTIR. The absorption spectrum of the N-CQDs was analyzed within a range of 200 to 800 nm. Fig. 1 a shows two absorbance bands at 240 nm and 360 nm that are due to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of C = C [40] and C = O or surface defects of N-CQDs [41]. The aqueous solution of N-CQDs was a dark brown solution in visible light and emitted fluorescence with a wavelength of 450 nm in that region. These results are in good agreement with the experimental data of the previous study [37].

TEM images revealed that the particles were mostly spherical and not compact (Fig. 1b). The DLS histogram indicated that the size distribution of the N-CQDs ranged from 1 to 6 nm (Fig. 1c). The XRD analysis showed a broad peak at 2θ = 20° (Fig. 1d), which corresponds to the typical graphite reflection of (002) [42]. The estimated crystal size of the N-CQDs was 0.82 nm based on Scherrer's equation. The FTIR analysis (Fig. 1e) indicated the presence of various bonds, including C-O, C-N, CO-NH, and C-H. The stretching vibration bands of N-H and O-H were also observed in the range of 3050–3500 cm−1.

Additionally, a fluorescence lifetime test was conducted using the Edinburgh FLS 1000 lifetime and steady-state spectrometer. The fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs were examined, as illustrated in Fig. 1(f). The average calculated fluorescence lifetime for the synthesized N-CQDs was found to be 3.92 ns.

Fig. 2 displays the RLS spectra of the N-CQDs probe in the presence and absence of fluvoxamine, which both spectra exhibit similar shapes with a maximum light-scattering wavelength of 434 nm. The intensity of RLS was found to increase at all wavelengths after the addition of fluvoxamine. The observed increase in Resonance Light Scattering (RLS) intensity, as documented in a prior study, correlates with the augmentation of particle size [43]. The mechanism underlying fluvoxamine detection relies on the interaction between the N-CQDs probe and fluvoxamine molecules. This interaction results in the formation of a complex between fluvoxamine and N-CQDs. Importantly, this complex formation leads to the generation of scattering particles with a larger diameter, ultimately contributing to the enhancement in RLS intensity. A similar mechanism has been reported in the literature [44,45].

To optimize the RLS intensity of the N-CQDs system, parameters such as pH, ultrasonication time, and probe concentration were varied and tested individually using the one-parameter-at-a-time method. The results of these tests are discussed in the following sections.

The effect of pH solution on RLS emission intensity of mixture of a constant concentration of probe (40.0 mgL-1) in the absence and presence of fluvoxamine (1.0 mgL-1) in BRB solution with different pH ranging from 3 to 10 after 5 min sonication at room temperature was studied by the recording of their RLS spectra. In Fig. 3 , the difference in RLS intensity before and after adding fluvoxamine (ΔIRLS) at 434 nm was plotted as a function of the pH solution. As it is seen, the ΔIRLS was increased by increasing pH up to 6 and then remained almost constant in the pH range of 6 to 8 and after a further increase in pH it was decreased. Therefore, biological pH of 7 was selected as the optimum pH.

The probe's pHpzc (point of zero charges) was determined by preparing suspensions of 30 mg of N-CQDs and 30 mL of 0.01 mol/L NaNO3 in separate bottles, adjusting the pH of the solutions to a range of 3–10 using HNO3 and/or NaOH solutions, and testing the final pH after 24 h of shaking. The pHpzc was determined to be the point where the pHinitial versus pHfinal curve intersected with pHinitial equal to pHfinal. The pKa value of fluvoxamine was considered, indicating that its protonated form is predominant at pH < 8.7, while its neutral form is predominant at pH > 8.7. Additionally, the probe's pHPZC was calculated to be 6, indicating that its surface charge is negative at pH > 6 and positive at pH < 6. Therefore, electrostatic interactions may be responsible for the observed increase in RLS. Different buffer solutions, such as BRB, phosphate, and acetate, were investigated, and the highest intensity of fluvoxamine was obtained using Britton-Robinson buffer at pH = 7.0.

This section describes an investigation into the effect of probe concentration on its RLS intensity. To conduct this investigation, the difference in RLS intensity before and after the addition of fluvoxamine (ΔIRLS) was plotted against the probe concentration (as shown in Fig. 4 ). The results indicate that ΔIRLS increased significantly with increasing probe concentration up to a maximum concentration of 60.0 mg L-1, after which it decreased at higher concentrations. This reduction in ΔIRLS at high concentrations may be attributed to the aggregation of probe particles, leading to self-quenching phenomena [46]. Therefore, a concentration of 60.0 mg L-1 was determined to be the optimal condition for further experiments.

This section aimed to investigate the impact of ultrasonication time on the RLS intensity of the probe in the presence and absence of fluvoxamine, over a period of 0–20 min. Ultrasonic waves were used to disperse the probe and increase the number of scattering centers, leading to an increase in the scattering signal. Additionally, reducing the agglomeration, increased the amount of adsorbed fluvoxamine, further boosting the scattering signal. The results in Fig. 5 showed that the ΔIRLS initially increased with increasing ultrasonication time, but after 12 min, further increases became insignificant. Hence, an ultrasonication time of 12 min was considered sufficient to reach quasi-equilibrium conditions.

To generate a calibration curve for measuring fluvoxamine, a series of solutions with different concentrations (at least nine concentrations) ranging from 0.01 to 12.0 mg L-1 were prepared at pH = 7 with a constant probe concentration of 60.0 mg L-1. All solutions were sonicated for 12 min at room temperature using an ultrasonic bath. Then, the changes in RLS intensity of the system at 434 nm were plotted as a function of fluvoxamine concentration (as shown in Fig. 6 ). The resulting calibration curve showed a linear response for fluvoxamine in the concentration range of 0.01–12.0 mg L-1. The calibration equation was ΔIRLS = 26.605C + 70.27 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9993. The method demonstrated high sensitivity with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5 µg/L. Additionally, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 1.13 % and 3.79 % for 2 and 0.05 mg L-1 fluvoxamine, respectively (n = 3), indicating the high accuracy of the method.

Once the optimal conditions were established, the method's selectivity was evaluated by examining the impact of different compounds on the quantification of 4.0 mg L-1 fluvoxamine. The results are summarized in Table 1 . The detection of fluvoxamine did not interfere with the presence of common ions, including Ca2+, Na+, k+, Cl-, and CO3 2–. In terms of the common interferences encountered in the detection of fluvoxamine in biological samples [47], varying concentrations of fructose, glucose, sucrose, tyrosine, and ascorbic acid did not have any significant impact on the accuracy of fluvoxamine detection. Similarly, the citalopram and sertraline drugs, two commonly prescribed antidepressant drugs with fluvoxamine [48], did not produce any observable interference in the detection of fluvoxamine. The results imply that the method is selective and suitable for the quantification of fluvoxamine in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of fluvoxamine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The method developed in this study was tested for its suitability for the analysis of real samples by measuring the fluvoxamine concentration in serum and drug tablet samples. In the case of serum samples, two samples were prepared by spiking with known concentrations of fluvoxamine (20 and 50 µg/L), and the results are presented in Table 2 . The mean recovery percentages were calculated based on three replicated measurements and found to be 97.0 % and 99.0 %, indicating very good accuracy. For the analysis of fluvoxamine tablets (50 and 100 mg), the results are summarized in Table 3 , and the average recovery percentages based on three determinations were 102.8 % and 98.5 %. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the developed method for the determination of fluvoxamine in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the efficiency of the present method with the reported methods
ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 provides a comparison of the analytical parameters of our newly developed method with those of previous methods used to measure fluvoxamine. Most of the previous methods are based on separation, which involves the extraction and pre-concentration of the analyte before measurement [7–9,49,50]. In contrast, our method is free from separation or pre-concentration steps. Despite the use of a relatively inexpensive and simple luminescence spectrometer. The proposed method has a lower or comparable limit of detection (LOD) compared to all of the previous methods. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, no previous study has reported on the direct measurement of fluvoxamine.

This study is the first to utilize N-CQDs nanoparticles as probes for measuring fluvoxamine using the RLS method. The findings indicate that the interaction between the compound and the probe increases the RLS intensity of the system at pH = 7. A comparison of the analytical parameters of the proposed methods demonstrated that it is a direct and cost-effective approach with comparable or higher sensitivity than the reported methods for measuring fluvoxamine.

Fariba Fazl: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Resources. Mohammad Bagher Gholivand: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Lorenzo, Microextraction by packed sorbent followed by ultra high performance liquid chromatography for the fast extraction and determination of six antidepressants in urine, Journal of separation science, 42 (2019) 2053-2061.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mohammad Bagher Gholivand has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at Shiraz University in 1993. He is currently a professor of chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran. His main research has been mainly focused on electrochemical sensors and biosensors. He has published over 180 scientific papers in international journals.

Fariba Fazl has received her B.Sc. in Chemistry from Razi University, Kermanshah in 2011, also her M.Sc. in Analytical Chemistry from Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan in 2014. During these years, she could successfully publish 3 scientific papers through international journals. She is currently working towards her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Mohammad Bagher Gholivand. Her research interests are electrochemical sensors.
